threats.title: Deterministic Underflow in remove_gauge Prevents Gauge Removal
threats.hypothesis_description: The remove_gauge function sets gauge_types_[_gauge] to 0 before calling _remove_gauge_weight. Inside _remove_gauge_weight, subtracting 1 from the now-zero gauge type causes an underflow to -1, which subsequently causes an underflow in the sum calculation, reverting the transaction and making gauge removal impossible.
threats.investigation_rationale: Code review shows state mutation ordering flaw where dependent function reads corrupted state.

proof_of_signal.pattern: State zeroed before being read by internal function, causing underflow

preconditions: Governance attempts to remove a gauge
Gauge has a positive weight bias

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: State should be read before being zeroed

analysis_questions: Does the underflow always cause a revert?
Can a gauge with 0 weight bypass the revert but corrupt type -1 state?
validation_requirements: Test remove_gauge with a voted gauge
Test remove_gauge with an unvoted gauge

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Governance calls remove_gauge on a malicious or deprecated gauge. The transaction reverts, leaving the gauge active and continuing to receive emissions.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Emissions cannot be redirected from deprecated gauges
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade (which is not supported)

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for gauge removal with active votes

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Easily detectable via failed governance transactions

threats.title: uint128 Overflow in accCantoPerShare Permanently Bricks Markets with Low Supply
threats.hypothesis_description: The accCantoPerShare accumulation formula divides by marketSupply and casts the result to uint128. If marketSupply is extremely small (e.g., 1 wei), the accumulated value per block is massive, causing the uint128 cast to overflow and revert in a short timeframe.
threats.investigation_rationale: Integer division by a user-controlled small denominator combined with a strict downcast creates a deterministic overflow vector.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Division by marketSupply inside uint128 cast without overflow protection

preconditions: Market supply is extremely low (e.g., 1 wei)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: If marketSupply is 1, the value exceeds uint128.max (~3.4e38) in ~340 blocks
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No minimum market supply enforcement or safe cast handling

analysis_questions: How quickly does the overflow occur with realistic cantoPerBlock values?
Does the revert block all subsequent deposits and withdrawals?
validation_requirements: Simulate 1 wei deposit and advance blocks
Verify sync_ledger reverts after overflow threshold

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker deposits 1 wei into a newly whitelisted market. After ~30 minutes, update_market reverts due to overflow. The market is permanently bricked, preventing any legitimate users from depositing or withdrawing.
prerequisites: Ability to deposit a very small amount (1 wei)

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent lock of any funds deposited after the attacker, total DoS of the market
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users of the affected market
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None without governance intervention to change block time parameters to halt accumulation

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing edge case tests for extremely low market supply

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain as reverting sync_ledger calls

threats.title: Stale epochTime in update_market Loop Allows Retroactive Reward Manipulation
threats.hypothesis_description: In update_market, epochTime is calculated once based on the current block.number and used for all historical epochs in the catch-up loop. This applies the current gauge weight to all past un-updated epochs, allowing an attacker to retroactively steal rewards by increasing the gauge weight right before calling update_market.
threats.investigation_rationale: Loop invariant violation where a time-dependent variable is not updated per iteration, breaking historical accuracy.

proof_of_signal.pattern: epochTime calculated outside loop context but used inside loop for historical data

preconditions: Market has not been updated for at least one past epoch
Attacker has voting power to change gauge weights

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: epochTime uses block.number instead of loop variable i
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: gauge_relative_weight_write is called with the current time for all historical epochs
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: epochTime should be calculated based on i

analysis_questions: Can an attacker profitably manipulate weights and trigger update_market in the same block?
How much historical reward can be stolen?
validation_requirements: Simulate delayed update_market call
Change gauge weight and verify historical epochs use the new weight

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker identifies a market that hasn't been updated for several epochs. They use their veCANTO to vote heavily for this market, increasing its current weight. They immediately call update_market, which applies this new high weight to all past epochs, granting the attacker vastly inflated rewards.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Theft of CANTO emissions intended for other markets

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for delayed update_market with changing gauge weights

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Sufficient veCANTO to move gauge weights
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Atomic execution of vote and update_market
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible as sudden spikes in accCantoPerShare

threats.title: Underflow in vote_for_gauge_weights Permanently Locks User Voting Power for Removed Gauges
threats.hypothesis_description: When a gauge is removed, users must vote 0 to reclaim their allocated voting power. However, voting 0 on a removed gauge causes gauge_types_[_gauge_addr] - 1 to underflow to -1, failing the subsequent require(gauge_type >= 0) check. The user's voting power remains permanently locked to the dead gauge.
threats.investigation_rationale: Contradictory validation logic where the first check explicitly allows 0-weight votes on removed gauges, but the subsequent array index calculation underflows and reverts.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(_user_weight == 0 || gauge_types_ != 0) followed by gauge_types_ - 1 underflow

preconditions: User has allocated voting power to a gauge

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: If gauge_types_ is 0, gauge_type is -1, which reverts, preventing users from reclaiming power
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should bypass gauge_type calculation if reclaiming power from removed gauge

analysis_questions: Is there any alternative way for users to reclaim their voting power?
Does this affect all users who voted for the removed gauge?
validation_requirements: Vote for gauge, remove gauge, attempt to vote 0
Verify transaction reverts and vote_user_power is not restored

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Governance removes a deprecated gauge. All users who had active votes for this gauge attempt to set their vote to 0 to reallocate their power. The transactions revert, permanently reducing their maximum voting capacity.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent loss of governance influence for affected users
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users who voted for the removed gauge

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for user vote reclamation after gauge removal

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Easily detectable via reverting vote transactions

threats.title: Early Withdrawal via unlockOverride Fails to Clear Gauge Votes, Allowing Infinite Voting Power
threats.hypothesis_description: If governance enables unlockOverride, users can withdraw their locked CANTO early. However, the withdraw function does not notify the GaugeController. The user's gauge votes remain active until the original lock expiration. The user can repeatedly lock, vote, and withdraw to infinitely multiply their gauge weight with the same capital.
threats.investigation_rationale: Cross-contract state synchronization failure where early termination of a lock in the parent contract is not propagated to the dependent voting contract.

proof_of_signal.pattern: withdraw clears local lock but does not interact with GaugeController

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: GaugeController assumes votes decay based on lock end, but the lock is destroyed early
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: GaugeController needs a mechanism to invalidate votes if the lock is withdrawn early

analysis_questions: Can the user vote for the same gauge multiple times using different addresses?
Does the GaugeController checkpointing eventually clear the phantom votes?
validation_requirements: Enable unlockOverride
Lock, vote, withdraw, transfer to new address, repeat
Verify gauge weight increases infinitely

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Governance enables unlockOverride for an emergency migration. An attacker locks 100 CANTO, votes for their gauge, withdraws the 100 CANTO, transfers it to a second address, and repeats the process 1000 times. They artificially inflate their gauge's weight to capture all protocol emissions.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Total capture of protocol emissions by the attacker
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires governance to disable unlockOverride and manually fix gauge weights

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing integration tests for unlockOverride interacting with GaugeController

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Looping attack requires gas, but highly profitable
technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Minimal capital required due to reuse
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Can be executed atomically across multiple addresses

threats.title: Inherited ERC20Burnable Methods Destroy Gauge Tokens Without Returning Underlying Assets
threats.hypothesis_description: LiquidityGauge inherits ERC20Burnable, exposing public burn() and burnFrom() functions. Calling these functions destroys the gauge tokens and reduces the user's ledger balance via the _afterTokenTransfer hook, but fails to transfer the underlying tokens back to the user, permanently locking them in the contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: Dangerous inheritance exposes standard token burning interfaces that bypass the protocol's custom withdrawal logic, breaking the 1:1 backing invariant.
preconditions: User or integrating contract calls burn() instead of withdrawUnderlying()

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Inheritance of ERC20Burnable without overriding burn methods
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: burn() triggers _afterTokenTransfer which syncs the ledger negatively, but never calls safeTransfer for the underlying token
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: burn() and burnFrom() should be disabled or overridden to return underlying tokens

analysis_questions: Are there any scenarios where burn() is intentionally used?
Can this be exploited by an attacker to grief other users?
validation_requirements: Deposit underlying tokens
Call burn()
Verify gauge tokens are destroyed, ledger updated, but underlying tokens remain in contract

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A user mistakenly calls burn() directly, or a third-party yield aggregator integration uses the standard burn() interface expecting a withdrawal. The gauge tokens are destroyed, and the user permanently loses their underlying deposit.
prerequisites: User interaction with standard ERC20 burn interfaces

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Direct loss of user principal (underlying tokens)
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None, underlying tokens are permanently stuck

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests verifying behavior of inherited burn functions

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible as gauge contract holding more underlying than its totalSupply

threats.title: Missing Delegation Functions Render Core Protocol Feature Unusable
threats.hypothesis_description: The VotingEscrow contract defines delegation state variables (delegatee, delegated) and includes complex logic in increaseAmount and withdraw to handle delegated locks. However, there are no external functions to actually delegate or undelegate voting power, rendering the entire delegation subsystem dead code and breaking a core governance feature.
threats.investigation_rationale: Architectural incompleteness where state variables and conditional logic exist for a feature, but the entry points to trigger that state transition are missing.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (delegatee == msg.sender) else branch exists but delegatee can never be changed from msg.sender

preconditions: Users attempt to delegate their voting power

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Users cannot participate in delegation strategies, and the else branch in increaseAmount is unreachable

analysis_questions: Was delegation intentionally removed but incompletely scrubbed?
Does the dead code introduce any other vulnerabilities?
validation_requirements: Verify there is no way to change the delegatee state variable

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Users expect to be able to delegate their veCANTO to governance aggregators or active voters. They discover the functionality is missing, preventing the formation of liquid governance markets or delegation strategies.

threats.title: Incomplete Checkpoint Loop Corrupts Global Voting Power After Extended Inactivity
threats.hypothesis_description: The _checkpoint function iterates a maximum of 255 weeks to fill the global point history. Since locks last 260 weeks (5 years), if the contract is inactive for more than 255 weeks, the loop terminates early. The next user action will add their current slope/bias to a stale global point from the past, permanently corrupting the global voting power math.
threats.investigation_rationale: Loop bound is strictly less than the maximum possible time horizon of active state (255 weeks vs 260 weeks), guaranteeing state desynchronization if the gap is hit.

proof_of_signal.pattern: for (uint256 i = 0; i < 255; i++) loop bound is less than LOCKTIME (260 weeks)

preconditions: No state-changing actions occur on VotingEscrow for > 255 weeks

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Loop breaks at 255 weeks, but global point is updated with current block timestamp's user bias
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Adding a present-day user bias to a 5-week-old global point corrupts the linear decay math
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Loop bound should be at least 261 to cover the full LOCKTIME

analysis_questions: Does the math corruption allow users to claim more voting power than they should have?
Can the state be repaired by subsequent checkpoints?
validation_requirements: Simulate 256 weeks of inactivity
Call createLock and verify global pointHistory math is corrupted

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: After 4.9 years of inactivity on a specific deployment, a user creates a new lock. The checkpoint loop processes 255 weeks and stops. The user's new voting power is added to the stale global state, resulting in a permanently inflated or corrupted global totalSupply.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Corruption of global voting power, affecting gauge weight distributions

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing long-term time-travel tests exceeding 255 weeks

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible as sudden jumps in totalSupply

threats.title: Re-whitelisting a Market Resets lastRewardBlock, Permanently Deleting Pending Rewards
threats.hypothesis_description: When governance calls whiteListLendingMarket with _isWhiteListed = true, it unconditionally sets marketInfo[_market].lastRewardBlock = uint64(block.number). If a market had accumulated rewards but was temporarily delisted (or if governance just calls it again), resetting lastRewardBlock wipes out all pending reward accumulation for the un-updated period.
threats.investigation_rationale: State overwrite without prior settlement. Modifying the time-tracking cursor without processing the elapsed time destroys the accrued value.

proof_of_signal.pattern: marketInfo[_market].lastRewardBlock = uint64(block.number) without calling update_market first

preconditions: Governance calls whiteListLendingMarket for the market

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Fails to call update_market(_market) before resetting the reward cursor
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should call update_market before changing whitelist status or resetting lastRewardBlock

analysis_questions: Can governance accidentally wipe out weeks of rewards by re-whitelisting?
Is there any way to recover the lost rewards?
validation_requirements: Accumulate rewards for a market without updating
Call whiteListLendingMarket(true)
Verify pending rewards are permanently lost

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Governance decides to re-whitelist a temporarily suspended market, or accidentally calls the whitelist function again. The lastRewardBlock is reset to the current block, instantly deleting all rewards that users earned during the un-updated period prior to the call.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of earned CANTO rewards for liquidity providers
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Liquidity providers in the affected market

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for re-whitelisting markets with pending rewards

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible as a jump in lastRewardBlock without corresponding accCantoPerShare increase
